# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when .beads/ detected

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
- `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

### Sync & Collaboration
- `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
- `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
- `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

### Project Health
- `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
- `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

○ long-running-harness-3yf [EPIC] · E5: Session Persistence & Undo Framework   [● P1 · OPEN]
Owner: Nathan Delacrétaz · Type: epic
Created: 2026-03-28 · Updated: 2026-03-28

DESCRIPTION
Zod session schema with version field for all session state (R-UBI-11). Draft-then-swap IndexedDB persistence pattern (write session_draft, on success rename to session_current, backup to session_backup). Save queue serialization (max one IDB write in-flight, INV-4). Auto-save every 30s with debounce during rapid edits. Session recovery dialog for malformed JSON (R-UNW-08): parse each section independently, load valid, discard invalid. Undo/redo command framework: Command interface (type, execute, undo, serialize). Command registry for domain epics to register command types. Bounded undo stack (200 entries) with buffer reference eviction. Session browser UI (list saved sessions, load, delete, rename). Ctrl+S keyboard shortcut. EARS: R-EVT-13, R-UBI-07, R-UBI-11, R-UNW-06, R-UNW-08, NFR-08, NFR-15. Interface exports: SessionSchema, Command, UndoManager. QA: round-trip serialization, concurrent save handling, schema migration.

DEPENDS ON
  → ✓ long-running-harness-8ew: E3: Transport & Scheduling Engine ● P0
  → ✓ long-running-harness-9sl: E1: Project Shell & Audio Infrastructure ● P0

BLOCKS
  ← ○ long-running-harness-tnf: (EPIC) E18: Integration Verification ● P1

1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
4	
5	## Project Map
6	
7	```
8	README.md             # Project overview
9	AGENTS.md             # YOU ARE HERE -- agent entry point
10	index.html            # Vite entry point
11	package.json          # Dependencies and scripts
12	src/
13	  main.tsx            # React entry point
14	  App.tsx             # Root component
15	  audio/              # Web Audio API engine (path alias: @audio)
16	  ui/                 # UI components (path alias: @ui)
17	  state/              # State management (path alias: @state)
18	  test/               # Test setup and utilities
19	docs/
20	  INDEX.md            # Documentation hub
21	  adr/                # Architectural Decision Records
22	  research/           # Deep research papers
23	  compound/           # Compound-agent documentation
24	.claude/
25	  CLAUDE.md           # Project instructions + agentic principles
26	  skills/             # Skill definitions (compound workflows)
27	  agents/             # Subagent definitions
28	  commands/           # Slash command definitions
29	  lessons/            # Session memory (NEVER edit directly -- use `ca` CLI)
30	.beads/               # Issue tracking data (Dolt-powered)
31	```
32	
33	## Stack
34	
35	- **React 19 + TypeScript 5.8** (strict mode, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`)
36	- **Vite 8** for dev server and build
37	- **Vitest 4** for testing (jsdom environment, @testing-library/react)
38	- **ESLint 10** with typescript-eslint strict type-checked rules
39	- **Prettier** for formatting
40	- **Husky** pre-commit hooks (lint + format check)
41	- **GitHub Actions CI** (lint, format, test, build)
42	
43	## Build Commands
44	
45	```bash
46	pnpm dev              # Start dev server
47	pnpm build            # Type-check + production build
48	pnpm test             # Run tests once
49	pnpm test:watch       # Run tests in watch mode
50	pnpm test:coverage    # Run tests with coverage
51	pnpm lint             # ESLint + TypeScript noEmit
52	pnpm lint:fix         # Auto-fix lint issues
53	pnpm format           # Format source files
54	pnpm format:check     # Check formatting (CI)
55	pnpm check            # Full quality gate: lint + format + test
56	```
57	
58	## Path Aliases
59	
60	Use `@/`, `@audio/`, `@ui/`, `@state/` instead of deep relative imports. ESLint enforces this (no `../../../` allowed).
61	
62	## Conventions
63	
64	- **Issue tracking**: `bd` only. No markdown TODOs.
65	- **Session memory**: `ca search` before decisions, `ca learn` after discoveries.
66	- **Decisions**: Record in `docs/adr/` using the template. See [ADR-0001](docs/adr/0001-initial-tooling-choices.md).
67	- **Research**: Extensive docs in `docs/research/`. Send subagents there for context (`ca knowledge "topic"`).
68	- **Tests**: Write tests first (TDD). Tests live next to source files as `*.test.tsx`/`*.test.ts`.
69	- **Types**: Strict TS everywhere. No `any`. Explicit return types on functions.
70	
71	## Quick Reference
72	
73	```bash
74	bd ready              # Find available work
75	bd show <id>          # View issue details
76	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
77	bd close <id>         # Complete work
78	bd dolt push          # Push beads data to remote
79	```
80	
81	## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
82	
83	**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
84	
85	Shell commands like `cp`, `mv`, and `rm` may be aliased to include `-i` (interactive) mode on some systems, causing the agent to hang indefinitely waiting for y/n input.
86	
87	**Use these forms instead:**
88	```bash
89	# Force overwrite without prompting
90	cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
91	mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
92	rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
93	
94	# For recursive operations
95	rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
96	cp -rf source dest          # NOT: cp -r source dest
97	```
98	
99	**Other commands that may prompt:**
100	- `scp` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` for non-interactive
101	- `ssh` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` to fail instead of prompting
102	- `apt-get` - use `-y` flag
103	- `brew` - use `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` env var
104	
105	<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION profile:full hash:d4f96305 -->
106	## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
107	
108	**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
109	
110	### Why bd?
111	
112	- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
113	- Git-friendly: Dolt-powered version control with native sync
114	- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
115	- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
116	
117	### Quick Start
118	
119	**Check for ready work:**
120	
121	```bash
122	bd ready --json
123	```
124	
125	**Create new issues:**
126	
127	```bash
128	bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
129	bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
130	```
131	
132	**Claim and update:**
133	
134	```bash
135	bd update <id> --claim --json
136	bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
137	```
138	
139	**Complete work:**
140	
141	```bash
142	bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
143	```
144	
145	### Issue Types
146	
147	- `bug` - Something broken
148	- `feature` - New functionality
149	- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
150	- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
151	- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
152	
153	### Priorities
154	
155	- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
156	- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
157	- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
158	- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
159	- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
160	
161	### Workflow for AI Agents
162	
163	1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
164	2. **Claim your task atomically**: `bd update <id> --claim`
165	3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
166	4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
167	   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
168	5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
169	
170	### Auto-Sync
171	
172	bd automatically syncs via Dolt:
173	
174	- Each write auto-commits to Dolt history
175	- Use `bd dolt push`/`bd dolt pull` for remote sync

178	### Important Rules
179	
180	- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
181	- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
182	- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
183	- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
184	- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists

186	- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems

190	## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
191	
192	**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
193	
194	**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
195	
196	1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
197	2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds

203	   git push
204	   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
205	   ```
206	5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches

6	## Context
7	
8	This project needs issue tracking and session memory for AI agent collaboration from the start. Traditional tools (GitHub Issues, wikis) don't integrate well with agent workflows -- they require API calls, context switching, and lack dependency awareness.

10	## Decision
11	
12	- **beads** (`bd`) for issue tracking -- Dolt-powered, dependency-aware, agent-optimized with JSON output and `bd ready` for unblocked work detection.
13	- **compound-agent** (`ca`) for session memory -- captures lessons learned during development, provides semantic search over past decisions and mistakes.
14	
15	Both tools are CLI-first and operate entirely within the repo (no external services required).
16	
17	## Consequences
18	
19	- Agents can discover, claim, and complete work without leaving the terminal.
20	- Lessons persist across sessions, reducing repeated mistakes.
21	- Requires `bd` and `ca` CLI tools to be installed for contributors.
22	- Issue data lives in `.beads/` (Dolt), lessons in `.claude/lessons/` (JSONL + SQLite).

# Long-Running Harness

A harness for managing long-running AI agent tasks. Stack TBD.

## Project Structure

```
.claude/          # Claude Code configuration, skills, agents
docs/
  research/       # Deep research papers (design, development, B2C product)

- **beads** (`bd`) -- Issue tracking with dependency awareness. See `bd ready` for available work.
- **compound-agent** (`ca`) -- Session memory and lesson capture. See `ca search` / `ca learn`.

## Getting Started

```bash
bd onboard          # Set up issue tracking
bd ready            # Find available work
ca search "topic"   # Search project lessons

2	title: "State Management Architecture for Web Applications"
3	date: 2026-03-26
4	summary: A comprehensive survey of client-side and server-side state management architectures in modern web applications, covering the theoretical foundations in finite automata and reactive programming, the taxonomy of approaches from flux/redux through signals and CRDTs, and the emerging convergence toward fine-grained reactivity and local-first paradigms.
5	keywords: [state-management, web-applications, reactivity, client-state, server-state, signals]
6	---
7	
8	# State Management Architecture for Web Applications
9	
10	*2026-03-26*
11	
12	## Abstract
13	
14	State management is the central architectural concern of interactive web applications. Every user interaction, network response, navigation event, and background synchronization produces state transitions that must be captured, propagated, and rendered with correctness guarantees ranging from immediate UI consistency to eventual convergence across distributed replicas. The problem is deceptively complex: a modern single-page application may simultaneously manage ephemeral UI state (modal visibility, scroll position), form state under validation, cached server data subject to staleness and invalidation, URL-encoded navigation state, derived computations that must remain consistent with their sources, and persistent state that survives page reloads and must synchronize across tabs or devices. The design space for addressing these concerns has expanded dramatically since Facebook's introduction of the Flux architecture in 2014, producing a rich taxonomy of approaches that differ in their granularity of reactivity, consistency models, coupling to rendering frameworks, and assumptions about the boundary between client and server.
15	
16	This survey examines the full landscape of state management architecture for web applications as of early 2026. It traces the theoretical foundations in finite state machine theory, Harel statecharts, reactive programming models, and distributed consistency theory. It classifies the major architectural families -- unidirectional data flow stores, atomic/fine-grained reactive systems, proxy-based observable graphs, state machines and statecharts, server state caches, URL-driven state, and conflict-free replicated data types -- and analyzes each with respect to mechanism, evidence, implementation, and trade-offs. The survey synthesizes cross-cutting concerns including performance characteristics, developer ergonomics, server-side rendering compatibility, and framework coupling. It identifies open problems including the TC39 signals standardization effort, the convergence of client databases with reactive query engines, and the unresolved tension between normalized caches and document-oriented optimistic updates.
17	
18	The presentation is descriptive rather than prescriptive. No single approach dominates all dimensions of the design space, and the selection among architectures remains fundamentally a function of application requirements, team constraints, and the specific mix of state categories an application must manage.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 1. Introduction
23	
24	### 1.1 Problem Statement
25	
26	A web application's state is the totality of information that determines what the user sees and what the application will do next. This definition, while simple, masks extraordinary complexity. Consider a dashboard application: the currently authenticated user (authentication state), the selected date range (UI state encoded in the URL), the list of metrics fetched from an API five seconds ago (server/cached state), a half-completed filter form (form state), whether a tooltip is visible (ephemeral component state), a computed aggregate derived from the fetched metrics and the selected range (derived state), and user preferences stored in localStorage (persisted state). Each category has different consistency requirements, different lifetimes, different update frequencies, and different failure modes.
27	
28	The evolution of web applications from server-rendered pages with minimal client-side logic to rich single-page applications (SPAs) and now to hybrid server/client architectures (Next.js App Router, Remix, SolidStart) has progressively shifted state management complexity to the client. Where a traditional server-rendered application could rely on the HTTP request/response cycle and server-side sessions to manage most state, a modern SPA must maintain a coherent in-memory representation of application state across asynchronous data fetching, optimistic mutations, real-time subscriptions, client-side navigation, and offline operation.
29	
30	The consequences of poor state management architecture are well-documented in industry experience: inconsistent UI rendering from stale or duplicated state, cascading re-renders degrading performance, race conditions from concurrent asynchronous operations, lost user input from navigation or hydration mismatches, and maintenance burden from tangled state dependencies that make refactoring hazardous. These failure modes motivate the search for architectural patterns that provide correctness, performance, and maintainability guarantees.
31	
32	### 1.2 Scope
33	
34	This survey covers state management architecture for web applications running in browser environments, with attention to:
35	
36	- **Client state categories**: UI state, form state, component-local state, global application state
37	- **Server/remote state**: Cache invalidation, stale-while-revalidate, optimistic updates, mutation lifecycle
38	- **Global state patterns**: Flux/Redux unidirectional data flow, signals, atoms, proxies, stores
39	- **State machines and statecharts**: Finite automata applied to UI logic, XState and the actor model
40	- **URL-as-state**: Router-driven state, search parameter management, deep linking
41	- **Derived/computed state**: Memoization, selectors, computed values, dependency tracking
42	- **State synchronization**: Cross-tab communication, Web Workers, multi-device sync, CRDTs
43	- **Persistence layers**: localStorage, IndexedDB, OPFS, session storage, WASM-SQLite
44	- **SSR/hydration**: State serialization, hydration mismatches, server components, streaming
45	- **Emerging patterns**: Signals (Solid, Angular, Preact, TC39 proposal), fine-grained reactivity, client databases
46	
47	The survey covers approaches as implemented in the major JavaScript/TypeScript frameworks (React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, Solid) and their associated ecosystems through early 2026.
48	
49	### 1.3 Key Definitions
50	
51	**State**: Any data that, if changed, requires the application to update its behavior or rendered output.
52	
53	**Reactivity**: The automatic propagation of state changes to dependent computations and UI bindings, without explicit imperative notification by the programmer.
54	
55	**Granularity of reactivity**: The precision with which a reactive system identifies and updates only the specific computations or DOM nodes affected by a state change, ranging from component-level (React's model) to node-level (Solid's model).
56	
57	**Consistency model**: The guarantees a system provides about the relationship between writes and subsequent reads, ranging from strong consistency (every read reflects the most recent write) to eventual consistency (reads will eventually reflect all writes, but may transiently return stale values).
58	
59	**Memoization**: The caching of computed results keyed by their inputs, such that repeated invocations with identical inputs return the cached result without recomputation.
60	
61	---
62	
63	## 2. Foundations
64	
65	### 2.1 Finite State Machines and Statecharts
66	
67	The theoretical foundation for modeling UI behavior as discrete states with explicit transitions derives from automata theory and its extension by David Harel. A finite state machine (FSM) is a quintuple (Q, Sigma, delta, q0, F) consisting of a finite set of states Q, a finite input alphabet Sigma, a transition function delta: Q x Sigma -> Q, an initial state q0, and a set of accepting states F [Hopcroft et al. 2006]. FSMs model systems where behavior depends not only on current inputs but on the history of prior inputs as encoded in the current state.
68	
69	Harel's statecharts [Harel 1987] extend classical FSMs with three critical features for modeling complex interactive systems: **hierarchy** (states can contain substates, enabling abstraction and reducing the state explosion problem), **orthogonality** (independent concurrent state regions that execute in parallel without requiring the Cartesian product of their state spaces), and **broadcast communication** (events can trigger transitions across orthogonal regions). These extensions address the central limitation of flat FSMs for UI modeling: a realistic interface with n independent binary features would require 2^n states in a flat FSM but only n orthogonal regions in a statechart.
70	
71	The application of statecharts to UI modeling is grounded in the observation that many UI bugs arise from implicit, unmodeled states. A data fetching component may be idle, loading, displaying data, or displaying an error -- but without explicit state modeling, intermediate states (loading while stale data is displayed) and impossible transitions (error-to-loading without a retry action) become sources of defects. The statechart formalism makes the set of valid states and transitions explicit and exhaustive.
72	
73	### 2.2 Reactive Programming Models
74	
75	Reactive programming provides the theoretical framework for automatic change propagation that underlies all modern state management systems. The field distinguishes several reactive paradigms relevant to web applications:
76	
77	**Functional Reactive Programming (FRP)**, introduced by Elliott and Hudak [1997], models time-varying values as continuous functions of time (behaviors) and discrete occurrences (events). While pure FRP is rarely implemented directly in web frameworks due to its continuous-time semantics, its conceptual influence pervades the field, particularly the separation of declarative state definitions from imperative side effects.
78	
79	**Transparent Reactive Programming (TRP)**, as embodied by MobX [Weststrate 2016], tracks dependencies at runtime by intercepting property accesses during computation execution. The key insight, articulated by Michel Weststrate, is that "anything that can be derived from the application state, should be derived -- automatically" [Weststrate 2016]. TRP systems maintain a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of dependencies between observable values, computed derivations, and side-effecting reactions. The critical invariant is **glitch-freedom**: no derivation ever observes an inconsistent intermediate state during a batch of updates.
80	
81	**Signal-based reactivity**, as pioneered by Solid.js [Carniato 2021] and now adopted across the framework ecosystem, refines TRP with three primitive constructs: **signals** (mutable reactive values with getter/setter interfaces), **derivations/memos** (cached computations that automatically track which signals they read and recompute only when those specific signals change), and **effects** (side-effecting functions that re-execute when their tracked dependencies change). The critical architectural distinction from virtual-DOM-based systems is that signals enable **fine-grained reactivity**: when a signal changes, only the specific DOM text nodes or attributes that depend on that signal are updated, without re-executing component functions or diffing virtual trees [Carniato 2021].
82	
83	The reactive evaluation algorithm common to signal-based systems operates as a hybrid push/pull mechanism. When a signal is written, dependent computations are **pushed** into a "dirty" state. However, the actual recomputation is **pulled** -- it occurs only when a dirty computation's value is read, either by the rendering system or by another computation. This lazy evaluation avoids unnecessary work when dirty computations are not currently observed. The topological ordering of the dependency DAG ensures that computations are evaluated in dependency order, preventing glitches [TC39 Signals Proposal 2024].
84	
85	### 2.3 Consistency Models
86	
87	State management architectures implicitly or explicitly adopt consistency models that determine the guarantees applications can rely on:
88	
89	**Strong consistency** ensures that every read reflects the most recent write. Local, synchronous state management (React's `useState`, Svelte's `$state`) provides strong consistency within a single browser tab by construction -- the reactive system ensures that all derivations and renderings reflect the latest state after each synchronous update batch.
90	
91	**Eventual consistency** guarantees that, in the absence of new writes, all replicas will eventually converge to the same state. This model is relevant when state must synchronize across tabs (via BroadcastChannel or localStorage events), across devices (via server synchronization), or across users in collaborative applications.
92	
93	**Strong eventual consistency (SEC)**, formalized through conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) [Shapiro et al. 2011], strengthens eventual consistency with the guarantee that any two replicas that have received the same set of updates (regardless of order) will be in the same state, without requiring conflict resolution. CRDTs achieve this through algebraic properties: state-based CRDTs use join-semilattice merge functions, while operation-based CRDTs require commutative update operations. Libraries such as Yjs [Nicolaescu et al. 2016] and Automerge [Kleppmann & Beresford 2017] implement CRDT-based state synchronization for web applications, enabling local-first architectures where the application operates offline and synchronizes state upon reconnection.
94	
95	**Stale-while-revalidate** is a caching consistency model, originally specified in HTTP Cache-Control headers (RFC 5861), that has been adopted as a state management paradigm by libraries including SWR and TanStack Query. Under this model, stale cached data is served immediately to the UI while a background revalidation request fetches fresh data. The UI may transiently display stale state, but responsiveness is prioritized over freshness -- a deliberate trade-off appropriate for read-heavy interfaces where data changes infrequently relative to access patterns.
96	
97	### 2.4 The CQRS and Event Sourcing Influence
98	
99	The Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) pattern, which separates read operations (queries) from write operations (commands), has influenced frontend state architecture in several ways. Redux's strict separation of action dispatching (commands) from state reading via selectors (queries) directly mirrors CQRS principles. Event sourcing -- the pattern of persisting state as an append-only log of events rather than mutable current state -- appears in Redux's action history (enabling time-travel debugging) and in CRDT operation logs. The Redux DevTools' ability to replay, skip, and reorder actions is a direct application of event sourcing principles to client-side state.

## Complete Codebase Exploration Report: Long-Running Harness

I've conducted a thorough analysis of the entire codebase. Here's the comprehensive picture for E5 planning:

### 1. PROJECT STRUCTURE

**Root organization:**
```
.
├── AGENTS.md              # Agent entry point (conventions, workflow)
├── package.json           # pnpm-based, 0.0.1
├── README.md              # High-level overview
├── index.html             # Vite entry point
├── tsconfig.json          # Project config references
├── tsconfig.app.json      # Main TypeScript config
├── src/                   # Main source code
├── docs/                  # Documentation and research
├── .claude/               # Claude Code integration
├── .beads/                # Issue tracking (bd/beads)
├── .github/               # CI/CD workflows
├── .husky/                # Pre-commit hooks
└── dist/                  # Build output
```

### 2. EXISTING STATE MANAGEMENT

**Current state architecture:**
- **Store**: `/src/state/store.ts` - Single Zustand store (`useDawStore`)
- **State categories managed**:
  - Transport: `transportState`, `bpm`, `cursorSeconds`, `loopEnabled`, `loopStart`, `loopEnd`
  - Audio Engine: `engineStatus`
- **Actions**: `play()`, `pause()`, `stop()`, `setBpm()`, `setCursor()`, `setLoop()`, `setEngineStatus()`

1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
4	
5	## Project Map
6	
7	```
8	README.md             # Project overview
9	AGENTS.md             # YOU ARE HERE -- agent entry point
10	index.html            # Vite entry point
11	package.json          # Dependencies and scripts
12	src/
13	  main.tsx            # React entry point
14	  App.tsx             # Root component
15	  audio/              # Web Audio API engine (path alias: @audio)
16	  ui/                 # UI components (path alias: @ui)
17	  state/              # State management (path alias: @state)
18	  test/               # Test setup and utilities
19	docs/
20	  INDEX.md            # Documentation hub
21	  adr/                # Architectural Decision Records
22	  research/           # Deep research papers
23	  compound/           # Compound-agent documentation
24	.claude/
25	  CLAUDE.md           # Project instructions + agentic principles
26	  skills/             # Skill definitions (compound workflows)
27	  agents/             # Subagent definitions
28	  commands/           # Slash command definitions
29	  lessons/            # Session memory (NEVER edit directly -- use `ca` CLI)
30	.beads/               # Issue tracking data (Dolt-powered)
31	```
32	
33	## Stack
34	
35	- **React 19 + TypeScript 5.8** (strict mode, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`)
36	- **Vite 8** for dev server and build
37	- **Vitest 4** for testing (jsdom environment, @testing-library/react)
38	- **ESLint 10** with typescript-eslint strict type-checked rules
39	- **Prettier** for formatting
40	- **Husky** pre-commit hooks (lint + format check)
41	- **GitHub Actions CI** (lint, format, test, build)
42	
43	## Build Commands
44	
45	```bash
46	pnpm dev              # Start dev server
47	pnpm build            # Type-check + production build
48	pnpm test             # Run tests once
49	pnpm test:watch       # Run tests in watch mode
50	pnpm test:coverage    # Run tests with coverage
51	pnpm lint             # ESLint + TypeScript noEmit
52	pnpm lint:fix         # Auto-fix lint issues
53	pnpm format           # Format source files
54	pnpm format:check     # Check formatting (CI)
55	pnpm check            # Full quality gate: lint + format + test
56	```
57	
58	## Path Aliases
59	
60	Use `@/`, `@audio/`, `@ui/`, `@state/` instead of deep relative imports. ESLint enforces this (no `../../../` allowed).
61	
62	## Conventions
63	
64	- **Issue tracking**: `bd` only. No markdown TODOs.
65	- **Session memory**: `ca search` before decisions, `ca learn` after discoveries.
66	- **Decisions**: Record in `docs/adr/` using the template. See [ADR-0001](docs/adr/0001-initial-tooling-choices.md).
67	- **Research**: Extensive docs in `docs/research/`. Send subagents there for context (`ca knowledge "topic"`).
68	- **Tests**: Write tests first (TDD). Tests live next to source files as `*.test.tsx`/`*.test.ts`.
69	- **Types**: Strict TS everywhere. No `any`. Explicit return types on functions.
70	
71	## Quick Reference
72	
73	```bash
74	bd ready              # Find available work
75	bd show <id>          # View issue details
76	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
77	bd close <id>         # Complete work
78	bd dolt push          # Push beads data to remote
79	```
80	
81	## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
82	
83	**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
84	
85	Shell commands like `cp`, `mv`, and `rm` may be aliased to include `-i` (interactive) mode on some systems, causing the agent to hang indefinitely waiting for y/n input.
86	
87	**Use these forms instead:**
88	```bash
89	# Force overwrite without prompting
90	cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
91	mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
92	rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
93	
94	# For recursive operations
95	rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
96	cp -rf source dest          # NOT: cp -r source dest
97	```
98	
99	**Other commands that may prompt:**
100	- `scp` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` for non-interactive
101	- `ssh` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` to fail instead of prompting
102	- `apt-get` - use `-y` flag
103	- `brew` - use `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` env var
104	
105	<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION profile:full hash:d4f96305 -->
106	## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
107	
108	**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
109	
110	### Why bd?
111	
112	- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
113	- Git-friendly: Dolt-powered version control with native sync
114	- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
115	- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
116	
117	### Quick Start
118	
119	**Check for ready work:**
120	
121	```bash
122	bd ready --json
123	```
124	
125	**Create new issues:**
126	
127	```bash
128	bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
129	bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
130	```
131	
132	**Claim and update:**
133	
134	```bash
135	bd update <id> --claim --json
136	bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
137	```
138	
139	**Complete work:**
140	
141	```bash
142	bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
143	```
144	
145	### Issue Types
146	
147	- `bug` - Something broken
148	- `feature` - New functionality
149	- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
150	- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
151	- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
152	
153	### Priorities
154	
155	- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
156	- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
157	- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
158	- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
159	- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
160	
161	### Workflow for AI Agents
162	
163	1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
164	2. **Claim your task atomically**: `bd update <id> --claim`
165	3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
166	4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
167	   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
168	5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
169	
170	### Auto-Sync
171	
172	bd automatically syncs via Dolt:
173	
174	- Each write auto-commits to Dolt history
175	- Use `bd dolt push`/`bd dolt pull` for remote sync
176	- No manual export/import needed!
177	
178	### Important Rules
179	
180	- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
181	- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
182	- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
183	- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
184	- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
185	- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
186	- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
187	
188	For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
189	
190	## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
191	
192	**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
193	
194	**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
195	
196	1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
197	2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
198	3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
199	4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
200	   ```bash
201	   git pull --rebase
202	   bd dolt push
203	   git push
204	   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
205	   ```
206	5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
207	6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
208	7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
209	
210	**CRITICAL RULES:**
211	- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
212	- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
213	- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
214	- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
215	
216	<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->

218	## Compound Agent Integration
219	
220	This project uses compound-agent for session memory via **CLI commands**.
221	
222	### CLI Commands (ALWAYS USE THESE)
223	
224	**You MUST use CLI commands for lesson management:**
225	
226	| Command | Purpose |
227	|---------|---------|
228	| `ca search "query"` | Search lessons - MUST call before architectural decisions; use anytime you need context |
229	| `ca knowledge "query"` | Semantic search over project docs - MUST call before architectural decisions; use keyword phrases, not questions |
230	| `ca learn "insight"` | Capture lessons - use AFTER corrections or discoveries |
231	| `ca list` | List all stored lessons |
232	| `ca show <id>` | Show details of a specific lesson |
233	| `ca wrong <id>` | Mark a lesson as incorrect |
234	
235	### Mandatory Recall
236	
237	You MUST call `ca search` and `ca knowledge` BEFORE:
238	- Architectural decisions or complex planning
239	- Patterns you've implemented before in this repo
240	- After user corrections ("actually...", "wrong", "use X instead")
241	
242	**NEVER skip search for complex decisions.** Past mistakes will repeat.
243	
244	Beyond mandatory triggers, use these commands freely — they are lightweight queries, not heavyweight operations. Uncertain about a pattern? `ca search`. Need a detail from the docs? `ca knowledge`. The cost of an unnecessary search is near-zero; the cost of a missed one can be hours.
245	
246	### Capture Protocol
247	
248	Run `ca learn` AFTER:
249	- User corrects you
250	- Test fail -> fix -> pass cycles
251	- You discover project-specific knowledge
252	
253	**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.
254	
255	### Quality Gate
256	
257	Before capturing, verify the lesson is:
258	- **Novel** - Not already stored
259	- **Specific** - Clear guidance
260	- **Actionable** (preferred) - Obvious what to do
261	
262	### Never Edit JSONL Directly
263	
264	**WARNING: NEVER edit .claude/lessons/index.jsonl directly.**
265	
266	The JSONL file requires proper ID generation, schema validation, and SQLite sync.
267	Use CLI (`ca learn`) — never manual edits.
268	
269	See [documentation](https://github.com/Nathandela/compound-agent) for more details.
270	<!-- compound-agent:end -->

1	# BRUTALWAV: Browser-Based Digital Audio Workstation
2	
3	## System Overview
4	
5	BRUTALWAV is a professional browser-based Digital Audio Workstation built on the Web Audio API. It provides multi-track audio and MIDI arrangement, a full-featured synthesizer, TR-808 drum machine (sample-based with tweakable parameters), effects processing, full mixer routing, modulation matrix, arpeggiator, and a piano roll editor -- all within a Neo-Brutalist interface that exposes its structural mechanics.
6	
7	**Target users**: Musicians, producers, and sound designers seeking a capable, browser-native production environment.
8	
9	**Tech stack**: React 19 + TypeScript (strict), Vite, Zustand (UI state), Web Audio API with native nodes for standard effects + AudioWorklet for custom DSP. Canvas/direct DOM for high-frequency rendering (metering, waveforms, piano roll).
10	
11	**Design direction**: Neo-Brutalist Expressionism with bold color accents. JetBrains Mono for data/values, system neo-grotesque for headings. 8px spatial grid. High-contrast black/white base with electric blue, hot pink, acid green, amber, and red accent palette.
12	
13	**Advisory profile**: `webapp` (data-dense, state-heavy, real-time interactive)
14	
15	**Build-great-things note**: All user-facing epics should invoke `/compound:build-great-things` during their work phase. This DAW requires excellence across all six build phases: Foundation (IA, state architecture), Structure (grid, responsive, component architecture), Craft (typography, color system, visual hierarchy), Motion & Interaction (micro-interactions, drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts), Performance & Polish (loading states, edge states, accessibility), and Launch (onboarding).
16	
17	---
18	
19	## EARS Requirements
20	
21	### R-UBI: Ubiquitous Requirements (always true)
22	
23	| ID | Requirement |
24	|----|-------------|
25	| R-UBI-01 | The system shall render all audio through the Web Audio API AudioContext |
26	| R-UBI-02 | The system shall use native Web Audio nodes (BiquadFilterNode, DynamicsCompressorNode, ConvolverNode, GainNode, etc.) for standard effects processing, and AudioWorklet processors for custom DSP (synth voices, custom envelopes, modulation routing). No ScriptProcessorNode. |
27	| R-UBI-03 | The system shall maintain separate audio-thread state and UI-thread state, communicating via lock-free mechanisms (MessagePort, SharedArrayBuffer) |
28	| R-UBI-04 | The system shall use sample-accurate scheduling via AudioContext.currentTime for all timed audio events |
29	| R-UBI-05 | The system shall preserve non-destructive editing: clips reference source audio without modifying originals |
30	| R-UBI-06 | The system shall apply the 8px spatial grid for all UI layout |
31	| R-UBI-07 | The system shall support undo/redo for all user-initiated edit operations via command pattern |
32	| R-UBI-08 | The system shall display real-time audio metering (peak + RMS) for all active tracks and master |
33	| R-UBI-09 | The system shall follow the Neo-Brutalist design system: JetBrains Mono for data, high-contrast palette, visible structural borders, exposed grid |
34	| R-UBI-10 | The system shall enforce TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess |
35	| R-UBI-11 | The system shall maintain all editable session state as a serializable JSON structure |
36	
37	### R-EVT: Event-Driven Requirements
38	
39	| ID | Trigger | Requirement |
40	|----|---------|-------------|
41	| R-EVT-01 | When the user presses Play | The system shall begin playback from the current cursor position, scheduling audio events using the look-ahead scheduler pattern |
42	| R-EVT-02 | When the user presses Stop | The system shall stop all audio playback, silence all outputs, and return the cursor to the last play-start position |
43	| R-EVT-03 | When the user presses Pause | The system shall suspend playback at the current position, retaining cursor position for resume |
44	| R-EVT-04 | When the user triggers a MIDI note (keyboard/mouse on piano roll or virtual keyboard) | The system shall allocate a synth voice, apply the current patch parameters (oscillators, filter, envelopes, LFOs), and route through the instrument track's signal chain within 10ms |
45	| R-EVT-05 | When the user imports an audio file (WAV/MP3/OGG/FLAC) | The system shall decode the file via AudioContext.decodeAudioData, store it in the media pool, and create a clip on the selected track |
46	| R-EVT-06 | When the user creates a new track | The system shall add a channel strip to the mixer (fader, pan, mute, solo, inserts, sends) and a lane in the arrangement view |
47	| R-EVT-07 | When the user adds an effect to a track insert slot | The system shall instantiate the corresponding AudioNode(s) and insert them into the track's audio graph chain |
48	| R-EVT-08 | When the user changes a synth parameter (oscillator type, filter cutoff, envelope, LFO rate, etc.) | The system shall apply the change in real-time via AudioParam scheduling with no audible glitch |
49	| R-EVT-09 | When the user clicks on the arrangement timeline | The system shall move the playback cursor to the clicked position, snapped to the current grid resolution |
50	| R-EVT-10 | When the user drags a clip on the arrangement | The system shall move the clip non-destructively, updating its timeline position and displaying ghost preview during drag |
51	| R-EVT-11 | When the user splits a clip | The system shall create two clips from the original, each referencing the same source audio with adjusted offsets |
52	| R-EVT-12 | When the user triggers a step on the 808 sequencer | The system shall play the corresponding drum sample (bass drum, snare, hi-hat, etc.) with user-adjustable parameters (tone, decay, tune, volume) via playback rate, amplitude envelope, and filter manipulation |
53	| R-EVT-13 | When the user saves the session | The system shall serialize the full session state to JSON and persist it to browser storage (IndexedDB) |
54	| R-EVT-14 | When the user initiates bounce/export | The system shall render the session to an audio buffer via OfflineAudioContext and offer download as WAV |
55	| R-EVT-15 | When the user activates the arpeggiator | The system shall auto-play held notes in the selected pattern (up/down/up-down/random) at the tempo-synced rate |
56	| R-EVT-16 | When the user adjusts tempo (BPM) | The system shall update the transport clock and reschedule all tempo-dependent events (metronome, arpeggiator, synced LFOs, step sequencer) |
57	| R-EVT-17 | When the user presses a keyboard shortcut | The system shall execute the mapped command without requiring mouse interaction |
58	
59	### R-STA: State-Driven Requirements
60	
61	| ID | State | Requirement |
62	|----|-------|-------------|
63	| R-STA-01 | While the transport is playing | The system shall advance the playback cursor, schedule audio events via the look-ahead scheduler, and update metering at 60fps |
64	| R-STA-02 | While a track is armed for recording | The system shall display the track in recording-ready state (hot pink accent) and monitor audio input if applicable |
65	| R-STA-03 | While loop mode is active | The system shall return the playback cursor to the loop start point when it reaches the loop end point, with seamless crossfade |
66	| R-STA-04 | While a track is soloed | The system shall mute all non-soloed tracks (solo-in-place behavior), except tracks with solo-isolate enabled |
67	| R-STA-05 | While the synthesizer is in legato mode | The system shall not re-trigger envelopes for overlapping notes, instead gliding pitch between notes |
68	| R-STA-06 | While the 808 sequencer is running | The system shall advance through the 16-step pattern at the current tempo, triggering drum sounds with accent and flam as programmed |
69	| R-STA-07 | While the arpeggiator latch is on | The system shall continue playing the arpeggio pattern even after all keys are released |
70	| R-STA-08 | While AudioContext is suspended (no user gesture yet) | The system shall display a clear prompt to click/interact to start audio, and resume the context on the first user gesture |
71	| R-STA-09 | While any parameter automation is active | The system shall apply automated parameter changes at sample-accurate timing, overriding manual knob positions |
72	
73	### R-UNW: Unwanted Behavior Requirements
74	
75	| ID | Condition | Requirement |
76	|----|-----------|-------------|
77	| R-UNW-01 | If audio output clips (exceeds 0dBFS) | The system shall activate the clip indicator on the affected channel and master, persisting until manually cleared |
78	| R-UNW-02 | If AudioWorklet processing exceeds the quantum budget (128 samples) | The system shall log the overrun event and continue processing without crashing |
79	| R-UNW-03 | If audio file decode fails | The system shall display an error with the file name and format, suggesting supported alternatives |
80	| R-UNW-04 | If browser tab loses focus during playback | The system shall continue audio playback uninterrupted (no throttling of audio thread) |
81	| R-UNW-05 | If the user attempts to create a feedback loop in routing | The system shall perform DFS cycle detection on the signal graph before applying any routing mutation, reject the command, and display a warning. This is a mandatory pre-condition for every addRoute, addSend, and addInsert operation. |
82	| R-UNW-06 | If IndexedDB storage is full during session save | The system shall notify the user and offer to export the session as a downloadable file |
83	| R-UNW-07 | If a synthesizer voice exceeds polyphony limit | The system shall steal the oldest or lowest-priority voice using the configured voice allocation strategy |
84	| R-UNW-08 | If the session JSON is malformed on load | The system shall display a recovery dialog and attempt to load as much of the session as possible |
85	
86	### R-OPT: Optional/Feature Requirements
87	
88	| ID | Feature | Requirement |
89	|----|---------|-------------|
90	| R-OPT-01 | Where MIDI input device is connected | The system shall accept MIDI note and CC messages, routing them to the selected instrument track |
91	| R-OPT-02 | Where the user enables sidechain compression | The system shall route the sidechain source signal to the compressor's detector input on the target track |
92	| R-OPT-03 | Where the user activates dark mode / light mode toggle | The system shall switch the full UI to the alternate Neo-Brutalist theme variant |
93	| R-OPT-04 | Where the user opens the modulation matrix | The system shall display all available modulation sources and destinations with drag-to-connect routing |
94	| R-OPT-05 | Where the user loads a preset | The system shall deserialize the preset JSON and apply all synthesizer/effect parameters atomically |
95	
96	---
97	
98	## Architecture Diagrams
99	
100	### C4 Context Diagram

100	### C4 Context Diagram
101	
102	```mermaid
103	C4Context
104	    title BRUTALWAV System Context
105	
106	    Person(musician, "Musician / Producer", "Creates music using the DAW")
107	
108	    System(brutalwav, "BRUTALWAV", "Browser-based DAW with synth, 808, mixer, effects, piano roll, arrangement")
109	
110	    System_Ext(webaudio, "Web Audio API", "Browser audio engine: AudioContext, AudioWorklet, AudioNodes")
111	    System_Ext(indexeddb, "IndexedDB", "Browser persistent storage for sessions and audio files")
112	    System_Ext(midi, "Web MIDI API", "External MIDI controller input")
113	    System_Ext(filesystem, "File System Access API", "Import/export audio files and sessions")
114	
115	    Rel(musician, brutalwav, "Composes, arranges, mixes, exports")
116	    Rel(brutalwav, webaudio, "Creates audio graph, schedules events, renders audio")
117	    Rel(brutalwav, indexeddb, "Persists sessions, presets, media pool")
118	    Rel(brutalwav, midi, "Receives MIDI note/CC input")
119	    Rel(brutalwav, filesystem, "Imports audio, exports WAV/project")
120	```
121	
122	### C4 Container Diagram
123	
124	```mermaid
125	C4Context
126	    title BRUTALWAV Container Architecture
127	
128	    Container_Boundary(browser, "Browser") {
129	        Container(ui, "UI Layer", "React 19 + TypeScript", "Arrangement, mixer, piano roll, synth editor, 808 pads")
130	        Container(state, "State Manager", "Zustand", "UI state, session model, undo history")
131	        Container(engine, "Audio Engine", "TypeScript + AudioWorklet", "Audio graph, scheduling, DSP processing")
132	        Container(transport, "Transport Controller", "TypeScript", "Play/pause/stop/loop, tempo map, cursor sync")
133	        Container(synth, "Synthesizer Core", "AudioWorklet", "Oscillators, filters, envelopes, LFOs, modulation matrix")
134	        Container(drums, "808 Drum Machine", "AudioWorklet + WebAudio nodes", "Analog-modeled drum synthesis + step sequencer")
135	        Container(effects, "Effects Rack", "WebAudio nodes + AudioWorklet", "Reverb, delay, compressor, EQ, distortion, chorus")
136	        Container(mixer, "Mixer Engine", "TypeScript + WebAudio", "Channel strips, routing, faders, pan, sends, master bus")
137	        Container(media, "Media Pool", "TypeScript + IndexedDB", "Audio file storage, decode, reference management")
138	    }
139	
140	    Rel(ui, state, "Reads/dispatches")
141	    Rel(state, engine, "Commands via MessagePort")
142	    Rel(engine, transport, "Clock sync")
143	    Rel(engine, synth, "Note events")
144	    Rel(engine, drums, "Trigger events")
145	    Rel(engine, effects, "Signal routing")

147	    Rel(ui, media, "Import/browse files")
148	```
149	
150	### Transport State Diagram
151	
152	```mermaid
153	stateDiagram-v2
154	    [*] --> Stopped
155	    Stopped --> Playing : play()
156	    Stopped --> Stopped : stop() [already stopped]
157	    Playing --> Paused : pause()
158	    Playing --> Stopped : stop()
159	    Playing --> Playing : seek() [reposition cursor]
160	    Paused --> Playing : play() [resume from position]
161	    Paused --> Stopped : stop()
162	    Paused --> Paused : seek()
163	
164	    state Playing {
165	        [*] --> NormalPlay
166	        NormalPlay --> LoopPlay : enableLoop()
167	        LoopPlay --> NormalPlay : disableLoop()
168	        LoopPlay --> LoopPlay : reachLoopEnd [jump to loop start]
169	    }
170	```
171	
172	### Synthesizer Voice Lifecycle
173	
174	```mermaid
175	stateDiagram-v2
176	    [*] --> Idle
177	    Idle --> Attack : noteOn(pitch, velocity)
178	    Attack --> Decay : attackComplete
179	    Decay --> Sustain : decayComplete
180	    Sustain --> Release : noteOff()
181	    Release --> Idle : releaseComplete [voice freed]
182	
183	    Attack --> Release : noteOff() [short note]
184	    Decay --> Release : noteOff() [medium note]
185	
186	    Idle --> Stolen : voiceSteal [polyphony limit]
187	    Stolen --> Attack : noteOn(newPitch) [reassigned]
188	```
189	
190	### Audio Signal Flow Sequence
191	
192	```mermaid
193	sequenceDiagram
194	    participant User
195	    participant UI
196	    participant Transport
197	    participant Scheduler
198	    participant AudioGraph
199	    participant Mixer
200	    participant Master
201	    participant Speakers
202	
203	    User->>UI: Press Play
204	    UI->>Transport: play()
205	    Transport->>Scheduler: start look-ahead loop
206	    loop Every 25ms (look-ahead)
207	        Scheduler->>Scheduler: Check events in next 100ms window
208	        Scheduler->>AudioGraph: Schedule source.start(time), noteOn(time)
209	    end
210	    AudioGraph->>Mixer: Per-track audio signal
211	    Mixer->>Mixer: Apply inserts, fader, pan, sends
212	    Mixer->>Master: Sum to master bus
213	    Master->>Speakers: AudioContext.destination
214	    Transport->>UI: Cursor position update (rAF)
215	```
216	
217	### 808 Step Sequencer Flow
218	
219	```mermaid
220	sequenceDiagram
221	    participant Sequencer
222	    participant Transport
223	    participant Scheduler
224	    participant DrumVoice
225	    participant Mixer
226	
227	    Transport->>Sequencer: Beat clock tick
228	    Sequencer->>Sequencer: Advance to next step (0-15)
229	    Sequencer->>Scheduler: Read pattern: which drums active on this step
230	    loop For each active drum on step
231	        Scheduler->>DrumVoice: triggerDrum(type, accent, time)
232	        DrumVoice->>DrumVoice: Synthesize (oscillator + noise + filter + envelope)
233	        DrumVoice->>Mixer: Audio output to drum bus
234	    end
235	```
236	
237	---
238	
239	## Scenario Table
240	
241	| # | Scenario | EARS Refs | Trigger | Expected Behavior | Verification |
242	|---|----------|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
243	| S01 | Basic playback | R-EVT-01, R-STA-01 | User presses Space (Play) | Audio plays from cursor, cursor advances, meters move | Audio output matches expected waveform via OfflineAudioContext |
244	| S02 | Stop and return | R-EVT-02 | User presses Space (Stop) while playing | Audio stops, cursor returns to start position | Transport state = Stopped, cursor = last start |
245	| S03 | Loop playback | R-STA-03 | Enable loop region, press Play | Seamless loop at boundaries | Rendered audio shows seamless transition at loop point |
246	| S04 | Synth note trigger | R-EVT-04 | Press key on virtual keyboard | Note sounds within 10ms, ADSR envelope shapes amplitude | FFT confirms correct pitch, amplitude envelope matches ADSR |
247	| S05 | 808 bass drum | R-EVT-12 | Trigger bass drum step | Characteristic 808 kick: sub-frequency punch with pitch sweep | Spectral analysis shows fundamental < 80Hz with descending pitch envelope |
248	| S06 | 808 pattern play | R-STA-06 | Activate 808 sequencer | 16-step pattern advances at tempo | Drum hits land on expected beat positions (sample-accurate) |
249	| S07 | Import audio | R-EVT-05 | Drag WAV file into arrangement | File decodes, clip appears on track | Decoded buffer matches source file, clip visible in UI |
250	| S08 | Add effect | R-EVT-07 | Add reverb to track insert | Audio passes through reverb processing | Wet/dry comparison shows reverb tail in output |
251	| S09 | Filter sweep | R-EVT-08 | Turn filter cutoff knob on synth | Real-time frequency change, no glitch | Spectrogram shows smooth frequency transition |
252	| S10 | Clip split | R-EVT-11 | Split clip at cursor | Two clips, same source, adjusted offsets | Both clips play identical content to original at their positions |
253	| S11 | Solo track | R-STA-04 | Solo track 2 | Only track 2 audible | Master output = track 2 output (within float precision) |
254	| S12 | Arpeggiator | R-EVT-15, R-STA-07 | Enable arp, hold chord | Notes play in pattern at tempo rate | Note timing matches expected pattern at BPM |
255	| S13 | Polyphony limit | R-UNW-07 | Play more notes than max polyphony | Oldest voice stolen, no silence gap | Continuous audio output, stolen voice fades |
256	| S14 | Clip indicator | R-UNW-01 | Push fader above 0dB with hot signal | Clip LED lights, stays lit | Peak meter shows clip, indicator persists |
257	| S15 | AudioContext resume | R-STA-08 | Open app fresh (no gesture) | Shows "Click to start audio" | AudioContext state = suspended until click |
258	| S16 | Feedback prevention | R-UNW-05 | Route track send to itself | Connection rejected, warning shown | Audio graph has no cycles |
259	| S17 | Session save/load | R-EVT-13, R-UNW-08 | Save session, reload page, load | Session restores identically | All tracks, clips, parameters match saved state |
260	| S18 | Bounce to WAV | R-EVT-14 | Select bounce, choose range | WAV file offered for download | Downloaded WAV matches real-time playback |
261	| S19 | Tempo change | R-EVT-16 | Change BPM from 120 to 140 | All tempo-synced elements update | Metronome, arp, LFO rates recalculate correctly |
262	| S20 | MIDI input | R-OPT-01 | Play note on MIDI controller | Note triggers on selected instrument | Same as S04, triggered via MIDI instead of mouse |
263	| S21 | Sidechain duck | R-OPT-02 | Setup sidechain: kick -> bass compressor | Bass ducks on kick hits | Amplitude analysis shows bass reduction coinciding with kick |
264	| S22 | Undo/redo | R-UBI-07 | Delete clip, then Ctrl+Z | Clip restored | Session state matches pre-delete state |
265	| S23 | Keyboard shortcuts | R-EVT-17 | Press 'M' on track | Track mutes | Track output = silence |
266	
267	---
268	
269	## Non-Functional Requirements
270	
271	| ID | Category | Requirement |
272	|----|----------|-------------|
273	| NFR-01 | Latency | Audio output latency shall be < 20ms from event trigger to audible output |
274	| NFR-02 | Performance | The system shall sustain 16 simultaneous tracks with effects at 60fps UI update rate |
275	| NFR-03 | Performance | Synthesizer shall support 16-voice polyphony without audio glitches |
276	| NFR-04 | Accessibility | All interactive controls shall be keyboard-navigable (WCAG 2.2 AA) |
277	| NFR-05 | Accessibility | prefers-reduced-motion shall be respected for all UI animations |
278	| NFR-06 | Browser Support | Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Safari 16+ (AudioWorklet baseline) |
279	| NFR-07 | Responsiveness | Minimum viewport: 1024px width (desktop-focused tool) |
280	| NFR-08 | Storage | Sessions up to 50 tracks shall save/load within 2 seconds |
281	| NFR-09 | Audio Quality | All DSP processing shall use 32-bit float at the AudioContext's native sample rate |
282	| NFR-10 | Testability | All DSP algorithms shall be testable via OfflineAudioContext (deterministic rendering) |
283	| NFR-11 | Deployment | The application server shall set Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp headers. App shall check crossOriginIsolated === true at startup and display hard error if false. |
284	| NFR-12 | Memory | Audio files shall use chunked streaming from IndexedDB/File System Access API. AudioBuffer used only for short samples (<30s). Long tracks use streaming or MediaElementAudioSourceNode. Imported file size limit: 500MB. |
285	| NFR-13 | Rendering | High-frequency UI updates (metering, waveforms, playback cursor, piano roll) shall bypass React state and render directly to HTML5 Canvas or mutate DOM via useRef, reading from SharedArrayBuffer via requestAnimationFrame. |
286	| NFR-14 | Audio Safety | AudioWorklet process() methods shall follow zero-allocation coding: all buffers pre-allocated at initialization, no object/array creation in the render loop. |
287	| NFR-15 | Data Integrity | Session JSON shall be validated against a Zod schema on load. Schema includes version field. Recovery loads valid portions and discards invalid data. Write uses draft-then-swap pattern with backup. |
288	| NFR-16 | Input Validation | All MIDI input values shall be clamped to valid ranges (note/CC: 0-127, pitch bend: 0-16383). SysEx messages capped at 1KB. All external input treated as untrusted. |
289	| NFR-17 | Export | Bounce/export shall use chunked OfflineAudioContext rendering (30-second blocks) with incremental WAV encoding to avoid single large buffer allocation. |

input.prompt:
Plan the implementation of E5: Session Persistence & Undo Framework for a browser-based DAW.

## Epic Requirements
1. Zod session schema with version field
2. Draft-then-swap IndexedDB persistence (write session_draft, rename to session_current, backup to session_backup)
3. Save queue serialization (max one IDB write in-flight)
4. Auto-save every 30s with debounce during rapid edits
5. Session recovery dialog for malformed JSON (parse each section independently)
6. Undo/redo command framework: UndoCommand interface (type, execute, undo, serialize)
7. Command registry for undo command types
8. Bounded undo stack (200 entries)
9. Session browser UI (list saved sessions, load, delete, rename)
10. Ctrl+S keyboard shortcut
11. Exports: SessionSchema, UndoCommand, UndoManager

## Current State
- Zustand store at src/state/store.ts (transport state, bpm, cursor, loop, engine status)
- IndexedDB pattern at src/audio/media-pool/idb-storage.ts (storage interface + in-memory test impl)
- Command registry at src/ui/keyboard/command-registry.ts (Command type with id, label, execute, enabled)
- ShortcutMap at src/ui/keyboard/shortcut-map.ts
- TypeScript strict mode, Vitest, NO Zod yet
- Path aliases: @/* -> src/*, @state/* -> src/state/*

## Constraints
- TDD workflow
- Follow existing patterns
- Minimal, lean code
- The existing Command type is for keyboard commands (no undo). The UndoCommand is a separate concept.

Create a concrete file-by-file implementation plan with clear ordering.

1	# BRUTALWAV: Browser-Based Digital Audio Workstation
2	
3	## System Overview
4	
5	BRUTALWAV is a professional browser-based Digital Audio Workstation built on the Web Audio API. It provides multi-track audio and MIDI arrangement, a full-featured synthesizer, TR-808 drum machine (sample-based with tweakable parameters), effects processing, full mixer routing, modulation matrix, arpeggiator, and a piano roll editor -- all within a Neo-Brutalist interface that exposes its structural mechanics.
6	
7	**Target users**: Musicians, producers, and sound designers seeking a capable, browser-native production environment.
8	
9	**Tech stack**: React 19 + TypeScript (strict), Vite, Zustand (UI state), Web Audio API with native nodes for standard effects + AudioWorklet for custom DSP. Canvas/direct DOM for high-frequency rendering (metering, waveforms, piano roll).
10	
11	**Design direction**: Neo-Brutalist Expressionism with bold color accents. JetBrains Mono for data/values, system neo-grotesque for headings. 8px spatial grid. High-contrast black/white base with electric blue, hot pink, acid green, amber, and red accent palette.
12	
13	**Advisory profile**: `webapp` (data-dense, state-heavy, real-time interactive)
14	
15	**Build-great-things note**: All user-facing epics should invoke `/compound:build-great-things` during their work phase. This DAW requires excellence across all six build phases: Foundation (IA, state architecture), Structure (grid, responsive, component architecture), Craft (typography, color system, visual hierarchy), Motion & Interaction (micro-interactions, drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts), Performance & Polish (loading states, edge states, accessibility), and Launch (onboarding).
16	
17	---
18	
19	## EARS Requirements
20	
21	### R-UBI: Ubiquitous Requirements (always true)
22	
23	| ID | Requirement |
24	|----|-------------|
25	| R-UBI-01 | The system shall render all audio through the Web Audio API AudioContext |
26	| R-UBI-02 | The system shall use native Web Audio nodes (BiquadFilterNode, DynamicsCompressorNode, ConvolverNode, GainNode, etc.) for standard effects processing, and AudioWorklet processors for custom DSP (synth voices, custom envelopes, modulation routing). No ScriptProcessorNode. |
27	| R-UBI-03 | The system shall maintain separate audio-thread state and UI-thread state, communicating via lock-free mechanisms (MessagePort, SharedArrayBuffer) |
28	| R-UBI-04 | The system shall use sample-accurate scheduling via AudioContext.currentTime for all timed audio events |
29	| R-UBI-05 | The system shall preserve non-destructive editing: clips reference source audio without modifying originals |
30	| R-UBI-06 | The system shall apply the 8px spatial grid for all UI layout |
31	| R-UBI-07 | The system shall support undo/redo for all user-initiated edit operations via command pattern |
32	| R-UBI-08 | The system shall display real-time audio metering (peak + RMS) for all active tracks and master |
33	| R-UBI-09 | The system shall follow the Neo-Brutalist design system: JetBrains Mono for data, high-contrast palette, visible structural borders, exposed grid |
34	| R-UBI-10 | The system shall enforce TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess |
35	| R-UBI-11 | The system shall maintain all editable session state as a serializable JSON structure |
36	
37	### R-EVT: Event-Driven Requirements
38	
39	| ID | Trigger | Requirement |
40	|----|---------|-------------|
41	| R-EVT-01 | When the user presses Play | The system shall begin playback from the current cursor position, scheduling audio events using the look-ahead scheduler pattern |
42	| R-EVT-02 | When the user presses Stop | The system shall stop all audio playback, silence all outputs, and return the cursor to the last play-start position |
43	| R-EVT-03 | When the user presses Pause | The system shall suspend playback at the current position, retaining cursor position for resume |
44	| R-EVT-04 | When the user triggers a MIDI note (keyboard/mouse on piano roll or virtual keyboard) | The system shall allocate a synth voice, apply the current patch parameters (oscillators, filter, envelopes, LFOs), and route through the instrument track's signal chain within 10ms |
45	| R-EVT-05 | When the user imports an audio file (WAV/MP3/OGG/FLAC) | The system shall decode the file via AudioContext.decodeAudioData, store it in the media pool, and create a clip on the selected track |
46	| R-EVT-06 | When the user creates a new track | The system shall add a channel strip to the mixer (fader, pan, mute, solo, inserts, sends) and a lane in the arrangement view |
47	| R-EVT-07 | When the user adds an effect to a track insert slot | The system shall instantiate the corresponding AudioNode(s) and insert them into the track's audio graph chain |
48	| R-EVT-08 | When the user changes a synth parameter (oscillator type, filter cutoff, envelope, LFO rate, etc.) | The system shall apply the change in real-time via AudioParam scheduling with no audible glitch |
49	| R-EVT-09 | When the user clicks on the arrangement timeline | The system shall move the playback cursor to the clicked position, snapped to the current grid resolution |
50	| R-EVT-10 | When the user drags a clip on the arrangement | The system shall move the clip non-destructively, updating its timeline position and displaying ghost preview during drag |
51	| R-EVT-11 | When the user splits a clip | The system shall create two clips from the original, each referencing the same source audio with adjusted offsets |
52	| R-EVT-12 | When the user triggers a step on the 808 sequencer | The system shall play the corresponding drum sample (bass drum, snare, hi-hat, etc.) with user-adjustable parameters (tone, decay, tune, volume) via playback rate, amplitude envelope, and filter manipulation |
53	| R-EVT-13 | When the user saves the session | The system shall serialize the full session state to JSON and persist it to browser storage (IndexedDB) |
54	| R-EVT-14 | When the user initiates bounce/export | The system shall render the session to an audio buffer via OfflineAudioContext and offer download as WAV |
55	| R-EVT-15 | When the user activates the arpeggiator | The system shall auto-play held notes in the selected pattern (up/down/up-down/random) at the tempo-synced rate |
56	| R-EVT-16 | When the user adjusts tempo (BPM) | The system shall update the transport clock and reschedule all tempo-dependent events (metronome, arpeggiator, synced LFOs, step sequencer) |
57	| R-EVT-17 | When the user presses a keyboard shortcut | The system shall execute the mapped command without requiring mouse interaction |
58	
59	### R-STA: State-Driven Requirements
60	
61	| ID | State | Requirement |
62	|----|-------|-------------|
63	| R-STA-01 | While the transport is playing | The system shall advance the playback cursor, schedule audio events via the look-ahead scheduler, and update metering at 60fps |
64	| R-STA-02 | While a track is armed for recording | The system shall display the track in recording-ready state (hot pink accent) and monitor audio input if applicable |
65	| R-STA-03 | While loop mode is active | The system shall return the playback cursor to the loop start point when it reaches the loop end point, with seamless crossfade |
66	| R-STA-04 | While a track is soloed | The system shall mute all non-soloed tracks (solo-in-place behavior), except tracks with solo-isolate enabled |
67	| R-STA-05 | While the synthesizer is in legato mode | The system shall not re-trigger envelopes for overlapping notes, instead gliding pitch between notes |
68	| R-STA-06 | While the 808 sequencer is running | The system shall advance through the 16-step pattern at the current tempo, triggering drum sounds with accent and flam as programmed |
69	| R-STA-07 | While the arpeggiator latch is on | The system shall continue playing the arpeggio pattern even after all keys are released |
70	| R-STA-08 | While AudioContext is suspended (no user gesture yet) | The system shall display a clear prompt to click/interact to start audio, and resume the context on the first user gesture |
71	| R-STA-09 | While any parameter automation is active | The system shall apply automated parameter changes at sample-accurate timing, overriding manual knob positions |
72	
73	### R-UNW: Unwanted Behavior Requirements
74	
75	| ID | Condition | Requirement |
76	|----|-----------|-------------|
77	| R-UNW-01 | If audio output clips (exceeds 0dBFS) | The system shall activate the clip indicator on the affected channel and master, persisting until manually cleared |
78	| R-UNW-02 | If AudioWorklet processing exceeds the quantum budget (128 samples) | The system shall log the overrun event and continue processing without crashing |
79	| R-UNW-03 | If audio file decode fails | The system shall display an error with the file name and format, suggesting supported alternatives |
80	| R-UNW-04 | If browser tab loses focus during playback | The system shall continue audio playback uninterrupted (no throttling of audio thread) |
81	| R-UNW-05 | If the user attempts to create a feedback loop in routing | The system shall perform DFS cycle detection on the signal graph before applying any routing mutation, reject the command, and display a warning. This is a mandatory pre-condition for every addRoute, addSend, and addInsert operation. |
82	| R-UNW-06 | If IndexedDB storage is full during session save | The system shall notify the user and offer to export the session as a downloadable file |
83	| R-UNW-07 | If a synthesizer voice exceeds polyphony limit | The system shall steal the oldest or lowest-priority voice using the configured voice allocation strategy |
84	| R-UNW-08 | If the session JSON is malformed on load | The system shall display a recovery dialog and attempt to load as much of the session as possible |
85	
86	### R-OPT: Optional/Feature Requirements
87	
88	| ID | Feature | Requirement |
89	|----|---------|-------------|
90	| R-OPT-01 | Where MIDI input device is connected | The system shall accept MIDI note and CC messages, routing them to the selected instrument track |
91	| R-OPT-02 | Where the user enables sidechain compression | The system shall route the sidechain source signal to the compressor's detector input on the target track |
92	| R-OPT-03 | Where the user activates dark mode / light mode toggle | The system shall switch the full UI to the alternate Neo-Brutalist theme variant |
93	| R-OPT-04 | Where the user opens the modulation matrix | The system shall display all available modulation sources and destinations with drag-to-connect routing |
94	| R-OPT-05 | Where the user loads a preset | The system shall deserialize the preset JSON and apply all synthesizer/effect parameters atomically |

253	| S11 | Solo track | R-STA-04 | Solo track 2 | Only track 2 audible | Master output = track 2 output (within float precision) |

270	
271	| ID | Category | Requirement |

Plan the implementation of E5: Session Persistence & Undo Framework for a browser-based DAW.

## Epic Requirements
1. Zod session schema with version field
2. Draft-then-swap IndexedDB persistence (write session_draft, rename to session_current, backup to session_backup)
3. Save queue serialization (max one IDB write in-flight)
4. Auto-save every 30s with debounce during rapid edits
5. Session recovery dialog for malformed JSON (parse each section independently)
6. Undo/redo command framework: UndoCommand interface (type, execute, undo, serialize)
7. Command registry for undo command types
8. Bounded undo stack (200 entries)
9. Session browser UI (list saved sessions, load, delete, rename)
10. Ctrl+S keyboard shortcut
11. Exports: SessionSchema, UndoCommand, UndoManager

## Current State
- Zustand store at src/state/store.ts (transport state, bpm, cursor, loop, engine status)
- IndexedDB pattern at src/audio/media-pool/idb-storage.ts (storage interface + in-memory test impl)
- Command registry at src/ui/keyboard/command-registry.ts (Command type with id, label, execute, enabled)
- ShortcutMap at src/ui/keyboard/shortcut-map.ts
- TypeScript strict mode, Vitest, NO Zod yet
- Path aliases: @/* -> src/*, @state/* -> src/state/*

## Constraints
- TDD workflow
- Follow existing patterns
- Minimal, lean code
- The existing Command type is for keyboard commands (no undo). The UndoCommand is a separate concept.

Ctrl+Shift+Z/Cmd+Shift+Z redo shortcuts. 66 new tests, all passing.
